summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews Direct Access, a productivity tool that saves time by replacing multi-step file/program opening sequences with short keyboard abbreviations. He demonstrates that the tool saved him 15 minutes during a 3-hour programming session, roughly a 10% productivity gain. He also highlights the tool's autotext feature for boilerplate code snippets, noting it's less useful in Ruby than Java due to less boilerplate. The post doubles as a self-reminder about salesmanship: showing users concrete ROI data is brilliantly simple marketing.
key_points: Small time savings from keyboard shortcuts compound significantly — 15 minutes saved in a 3-hour session represents a measurable 10% productivity boost
Showing users their own ROI data is brilliantly simple salesmanship, and McKenzie notes he should apply this to his own product's upgrade screen
Ruby/Rails requires far less boilerplate than Java, making code snippet macros less valuable but the program-launching shortcuts still highly useful
When you're the boss, every second of friction has a direct cost — unlike salaried employment where productivity losses are someone else's problem
money_quotes: You wouldn't think shaving a few seconds here and a few seconds there would really matter that much, but the stats don't lie.
I get paid regardless of whether I'm being productive or not at the day job. When I'm the boss, heck, time spent clicking is time spent not doing more important things.
The third time I type the same freaking snippet out it goes in Direct Access with a memorable abbreviation.
Scrape user accounts for Kalzumeus, generate similar ROI-proving eyecandy, and put at the top of the Upgrade Your Account screen. Its brilliantly simple salesmanship.
You can keep your OS X, vim, and bash, I'm puttering around in a deliciously iconoclastic Windows XP… at Unix sysop speeds.
As usual, product placements on this blog are 100% uncompensated. I like it, I use it, it saves me time and money, I blog about it.
key_insight: Small workflow optimizations that save seconds per interaction compound into significant productivity gains, and showing users concrete data about those gains is powerful salesmanship.
